stem: Let \( p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \) be [[n]] propositional variables, and consider the formula \( p_1 \vee \neg p_1 \) (only \( p_1 \) appears in it). Out of the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) possible truth assignments to \( p_1, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \), how many make this formula TRUE?
solution: \( p_1 \vee \neg p_1 \) is a tautology (the Law of the Excluded Middle) — exactly one of \( p_1, \neg p_1 \) is always true regardless of \( p_1 \)'s value, and the other [[n]]\( -1 \) variables are unrestricted, so ALL \( 2^{[[n]]} \) assignments make it true: \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly restricts only half the assignments, as if the formula depended on an extra free variable.
This treats the formula as a contradiction (always false) rather than a tautology.
This treats the formula as true for only a single assignment, rather than for all of them.
This confuses the number of variables with the count of satisfying assignments.
This misses exactly one assignment out of the total, as if one case failed.
This overcounts by one beyond the total number of possible assignments.

stem: Let \( p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \) be [[n]] propositional variables, and consider the formula \( p_1 \wedge \neg p_1 \) (only \( p_1 \) appears in it). Out of the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) possible truth assignments to \( p_1, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \), how many make this formula TRUE?
solution: \( p_1 \wedge \neg p_1 \) is a contradiction: \( p_1 \) and \( \neg p_1 \) can never both be true, so NO assignment (regardless of the other [[n]]\( -1 \) variables) makes it true: \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the formula as a tautology (always true) rather than a contradiction.
This mistakenly allows half the assignments to satisfy it.
This mistakenly allows exactly one assignment to satisfy it.
This confuses the number of variables with the count of satisfying assignments.
This allows all but one assignment to satisfy it, the opposite of a contradiction.
A count of satisfying assignments can never be negative.

stem: Let \( p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \) be [[n]] propositional variables, and consider the formula \( \bigl(p_1 \wedge (p_1 \rightarrow p_2)\bigr) \rightarrow p_2 \) (only \( p_1, p_2 \) appear in it). Out of the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) possible truth assignments to \( p_1, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \), how many make this formula TRUE?
solution: This formula encodes modus ponens: whenever \( p_1 \) is true and \( p_1 \to p_2 \) is true, \( p_2 \) must also be true, so the implication never fails. It is a tautology, true for the entire truth table — all \( 2^{[[n]]} \) assignments (the other [[n]]\( -2 \) variables are free): \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly restricts satisfaction to only half the assignments.
This confuses the formula with one that constrains both \( p_1 \) and \( p_2 \) to specific values.
This treats the formula as a contradiction rather than a tautology.
This is the true-count for the one-directional implication \( p_1 \to p_2 \) alone, not this whole tautology.
This misses exactly one assignment, as if the formula failed once.
This overcounts by one beyond the total number of possible assignments.

stem: Let \( p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \) be [[n]] propositional variables, and consider the formula \( (p_1 \leftrightarrow p_2) \vee (p_1 \leftrightarrow \neg p_2) \) (only \( p_1, p_2 \) appear in it). Out of the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) possible truth assignments to \( p_1, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \), how many make this formula TRUE?
solution: For any \( p_1, p_2 \), either \( p_2 \) agrees with \( p_1 \) (making \( p_1 \leftrightarrow p_2 \) true) or it disagrees (making \( p_1 \leftrightarrow \neg p_2 \) true) — exactly one of the two disjuncts always holds. This is a tautology, true for all \( 2^{[[n]]} \) assignments: \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly restricts satisfaction to only half the assignments, as though only one disjunct could ever be checked.
This confuses the formula with one requiring \( p_1 \) and \( p_2 \) to take specific values.
This treats the formula as a contradiction rather than a tautology.
This confuses the number of variables with the count of satisfying assignments.
This misses exactly one assignment, as if the formula failed once.
This overcounts by one beyond the total number of possible assignments.

stem: Let \( p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \) be [[n]] propositional variables, and consider the formula \( (p_1 \wedge p_2) \vee \neg p_1 \) (only \( p_1, p_2 \) appear in it). Out of the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) possible truth assignments to \( p_1, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \), how many make this formula TRUE?
solution: \( (p_1\wedge p_2)\vee \neg p_1 \) is logically equivalent to \( p_1 \to p_2 \), which is false only when \( p_1 \) is true and \( p_2 \) is false. It is neither a tautology nor a contradiction (it is contingent): it fails on \( 2^{[[n]]-2} \) of the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) assignments and holds on the remaining \( 2^{[[n]]} - 2^{[[n]]-2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly treats the formula as a tautology, true on every assignment.
This mistakenly treats the formula as satisfied on exactly half the assignments.
This is the count of assignments where the formula is FALSE, not where it is true.
This mistakenly treats the formula as a contradiction, true on no assignment.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Let \( p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \) be [[n]] propositional variables, and consider the formula \( p_1 \oplus p_2 \) (the exclusive-or of \( p_1 \) and \( p_2 \); only \( p_1, p_2 \) appear in it). Out of the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) possible truth assignments to \( p_1, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \), how many make this formula TRUE?
solution: \( p_1 \oplus p_2 \) is true exactly when \( p_1, p_2 \) disagree (2 of the 4 combinations), so it is neither a tautology nor a contradiction. With the other [[n]]\( -2 \) variables free, it is true for \( 2 \cdot 2^{[[n]]-2} = 2^{[[n]]-1} = [[answer]] \) of the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) assignments.
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly treats the formula as a tautology, true on every assignment.
This mistakenly restricts satisfaction to only a quarter of the assignments.
This mistakenly treats the formula as a contradiction, true on no assignment.
This confuses the number of variables with the count of satisfying assignments.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.
